claim.content: In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

conclusion.content: In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

premises.content:
In angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) of a crystalline solid, the component of the electron crystal momentum perpendicular to the sample surface, k_{\perp}, is commonly estimated from the incident photon energy h
u by the free-electron final-state approximation k_{\perp}=\sqrt{	frac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}} igl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}	heta + V_{0} igr)}, where m_{0} is the free-electron mass, \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} is the photoelectron kinetic energy measured with respect to the vacuum level (E_{\mathrm{kin}}=h
u-\phi-E_{B}, with \phi the sample work function and E_{B} the electron binding energy), 	heta is the electron emission polar angle, and V_{0} is an inner-potential model parameter; applying this mapping with the model parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} assigns the photon energy h
u=29\ \mathrm{eV} to the first bulk R point in the perpendicular momentum coordinate for CsPbBr_{3} in the measurements reported here.

For a valence band with energy dispersion E(k) as a function of crystal momentum k along a measured one-dimensional cut, the hole effective mass m_{h}^{*} at a local band maximum at k=k_{0} is obtained by fitting the local dispersion to a quadratic form E(k)\approx E(k_{0})+\tfrac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_{h}^{*}}(k-k_{0})^{2} in a neighborhood of k_{0}; in this expression \hbar denotes the reduced Planck constant, E(k_{0}) the band energy at the extremum, and k the momentum coordinate along the measured direction, and the extracted parameter m_{h}^{*} is the curvature-derived hole effective mass under the assumption that the actual band dispersion within the sampled momentum window is well approximated by a parabola.

The experimental uncertainty reported for the ARPES-determined hole effective mass, \pm 0.016\,m_{0}, is the sample-to-sample standard deviation of four independent fitted effective-mass values obtained from four different freshly cleaved single-crystal samples (individual fitted values 0.19, 0.23, 0.20, and 0.19\,m_{0}); this uncertainty is taken to represent the combined random and systematic uncertainty affecting the ARPES determination, where the principal systematic-error sources considered include photon-energy calibration, inner-potential selection, surface-condition variability after cleavage, analyzer angular calibration, and choice of fitting model, and the proposition is that these systematic contributions are either small compared to the observed sample-to-sample scatter or are effectively captured by the quoted standard deviation.

steps.reasoning:
State the experimental technique and sample conditions used to determine the valence-band maximum (VBM) and the hole effective mass: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements were performed on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr$_{3}$ at room temperature with photon energies varied between $h\nu=21\,$eV and $h\nu=83\,$eV in 2 eV steps to map the electronic states as a function of the electron wave vector perpendicular to the surface $k_{\perp}$, with spatial resolution $\sim 100\ \mu$m and base pressure better than $2\times10^{-10}$ mbar. 
Fig. 2

Define notation used for the measured quantity: denote the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum by $m_{h}^{*}$ and the free-electron mass by $m_{0}$; the photon energy is $h\nu$ and the perpendicular momentum is $k_{\perp}$.

Explain how the VBM (the R point) was identified from the photon-energy-dependent ARPES data: normal-emission (i.e., $\overline{\Gamma}$) photon-energy-dependent scans show symmetry about $k_{\perp}=3.66\ \text{\AA}^{-1}$ and the contours of constant binding energy near the VBM close at photon energies of $h\nu=29\,$eV and $h\nu=54\,$eV, which the authors therefore assign as the first and second R points; this assignment was corroborated by $k_{\parallel}$-dependent contour plots near 1.5 eV binding energy (near the VBM) and by the observation of closing contours at the specified photon energies. The assignment corresponds to an inner potential $V_{0}=5.3\,$eV for the R-point mapping (a different inner potential $V_{0}=9.6\,$eV was used for an X/$\Gamma$ assignment at normal emission). 
Fig. 2

State how the VBM photon energy was confirmed from the dispersion along the surface high-symmetry direction: band-dispersion cuts along $\overline{\Gamma}-\overline{M}$ were taken at multiple photon energies and the binding-energy of the local maximum (related to the VBM) was evaluated as a function of photon energy; these data confirm $h\nu=29\,$eV as the photon energy at which the measured band reaches the VBM (R point) and show that the dispersion at $29\,$eV appears narrower than at other photon energies. 
Fig. 3

Describe the procedure used to extract effective masses from the ARPES dispersions: for the band dispersion measured along $\overline{\Gamma}-\overline{M}$ at the photon energy assigned to the VBM ($h\nu=29\,$eV), the authors fit the measured dispersion of the topmost valence band near its maximum to obtain a hole effective mass $m_{h}^{*}$ (the fitting procedure and fit of the dispersion are those used to produce the plotted effective masses versus photon energy). 
Fig. 3

Report the repeat measurements and fitted individual effective-mass values: the measurement at $h\nu=29\,$eV was repeated on four different cleaved single-crystal samples, yielding individual fitted hole effective masses of $0.19\ m_{0}$, $0.23\ m_{0}$, $0.20\ m_{0}$, and $0.19\ m_{0}$; these four values are those obtained from the dispersion fits for the topmost valence band at the assigned R point. 
Fig. 3

Compute and report the average and uncertainty: the average of the four repeated fitted values $0.19\ m_{0}$, $0.23\ m_{0}$, $0.20\ m_{0}$, and $0.19\ m_{0}$ is $0.203\ m_{0}$ and the standard deviation is $\pm 0.016\ m_{0}$; therefore the experimentally determined hole effective mass at the VBM (R point) from ARPES is $m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\ m_{0}$. 
Fig. 3

motivating_questions.content: In the context of using ARPES to probe carrier quasiparticle properties in CsPbBr_{3}, prior experimental reports extracted VBM effective masses that differed substantially (for example, $0.26\;m_{0}$ in a previous ARPES study). These discrepancies can arise because ARPES effective-mass extraction requires precise locating of the VBM in three-dimensional momentum space (including $k_{\perp}$), because different photon energies probe different $k_{\perp}$ slices, and because inner-potential and final-state assumptions shift the assigned high-symmetry points. Without repeated, photon-energy-dependent mapping and cross-checked sample statistics, an assigned mass can be biased by off-VBM cuts or by sample-to-sample variation, preventing a reliable experimental benchmark for testing polaronic mass renormalization hypotheses.

paper:867765810263752934.authors: Maryam Sajedi | Maxim Krivenkov | Dmitry Marchenko | Jaime Sánchez-Barriga | Anoop K. Chandran | Andrei Varykhalov | Emile D. L. Rienks | Irene Aguilera | Stefan Blügel | Oliver Rader
paper:867765810263752934.en_title: Is there a polaron signature in angle-resolved photoemission of CsPbBr3?
paper:867765810263752934.zh_title: 在CsPbBr3的角分辨光电子能谱中是否存在极化子特征？
